coredata.dc:description: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), owing to their characteristic luminescent properties, have become a new favorite in the field of luminescence. They have been widely used in light emitting diode, ion detection, cell-imaging, ect. Herein a facile synthesis method of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) has been developedviaa one-step hydrothermal of glucose and m-phenylenediamine. The chemical composition, surface functional groups, and crystal structure of so prepared N-CQDs were systematically characterized. The characterizations indicate that nitrogen has been chemically doped in the CQDs and the N-CQDs crystallize in a graphene structure. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements show that the N-CQDs emit strong blue emission under the irradiation of ultraviolet. The emission is excitation-dependent, is resistant to photo bleaching and high ionic strength, and slightly decreases with the increase of temperature. The quantum yield of them is about 17.5%. The PL intensity of N-CQDs quenches linearly with the increase of the concentrations of Fe3+(0.5–1.0 mM) and CrO4 2−(0.3–0.6 mM), which are a kind of excellent fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ and CrO4 2−. The quenching mechanism of Fe3+ and CrO4 2−is verified to be a static quenching mechanism based on inner filter effect. The N-CQDs are also found to be a good cell-imaging reagent of Hela cells.

head.ce:title: Hydrothermal synthesis of N-doped carbon quantum dots and their application in ion-detection and cell-imaging

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, 26, Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, PR China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: School of Chemistry, Chemical Engineering and Resource Utilization, Northeast Forestry University, 26, Hexing Road, Harbin 150040, PR China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: Carbon quantum dots (CQDs), owing to their characteristic luminescent properties, have become a new favorite in the field of luminescence. They have been widely used in light emitting diode, ion detection, cell-imaging, ect. Herein a facile synthesis method of nitrogen-doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) has been developedviaa one-step hydrothermal of glucose and m-phenylenediamine. The chemical composition, surface functional groups, and crystal structure of so prepared N-CQDs were systematically characterized. The characterizations indicate that nitrogen has been chemically doped in the CQDs and the N-CQDs crystallize in a graphene structure. Photoluminescence (PL) measurements show that the N-CQDs emit strong blue emission under the irradiation of ultraviolet. The emission is excitation-dependent, is resistant to photo bleaching and high ionic strength, and slightly decreases with the increase of temperature. The quantum yield of them is about 17.5%. The PL intensity of N-CQDs quenches linearly with the increase of the concentrations of Fe3+(0.5–1.0 mM) and CrO4 2−(0.3–0.6 mM), which are a kind of excellent fluorescent probe for the detection of Fe3+ and CrO4 2−. The quenching mechanism of Fe3+ and CrO4 2−is verified to be a static quenching mechanism based on inner filter effect. The N-CQDs are also found to be a good cell-imaging reagent of Hela cells.

ce:section.ce:para: In recent years, carbon-based nanomaterials, such as carbon nanotubes, graphene, and fullerene, have attracted the attention of chemists and material scientists [1–6]. These materials have been widely used in catalysis, sensor, solar cell, ect. Nevertheless, they have no standing in the field of luminescent materials due to their conductive property [7–11]. In 2004, carbon quantum dots (CQDs) were discovered in the purification of single-walled carbon nanotubes, and the fluorescent properties of which were first detected, even though their fluorescence intensity was relatively weak [12]. This discovery triggered intensive researches on light emitting CQDs and their potential applications [13–21]. In the past few years, it has been verified that a chemical doping of heteroatoms, such as borium, silicon, nitrogen, sulphur, or phosphorus, into CQDs can effectively improve their fluorescence quantum efficiency [22–27]. Since nitrogen is adjacent to carbon in the periodic table, and can fit well at the lattice position of carbon-based materials, nitrogen doped carbon quantum dots (N-CQDs) have been greatly valued over other element doped CQDs [28–31].

The synthetic strategies of N-CQDs are based on either nitrogenization of CQDs or fusion of carbon precursor and nitrogenization agent [32–35]. The former approach, always a top-down approach, involves oxidative cutting, reductive cutting, physical grinding, or supersonic cutting of large graphitized carbon materials followed by nitrogenization [36–38]. The latter approach, usually a bottom-up method, includes a reaction of carbon precursor and nitrogenization agent followed by dehydration, aggregation (or polymerization) [39–42]. In comparison, the top-down method always utilizes low-cost raw materials, but it often requires long reaction time, has low production yield. The bottom-up method, however, possesses a mild reaction condition and higher production yield, more importantly, it is convenient to introduce heteroatoms into CQDs [43,44]. So, the bottom-up method is greatly favored in the synthesis of N-CQDs. Hydrothermal carbonization is traditionally a way to prepare carbon-based materials, hence It is certainly an approach to synthesize CQDs. It is greatly valued due to its convenience, low-cost, and mild reaction condition over other bottom-up methods.

A great many precursors have been used to synthesize CQDs, among which carbohydrate is greatly favored for its low cost, free availability, and environmental friendliness. CQDs prepared from carbohydrate, however, are always sp3 carbon-based CQDs [45–47]. Carbonyl is the basic functional group of glucose, which can react with amino to form CN group. Dehydrating such a nitrogenized glucose compound maybe is a rational approach to synthesize N-CQDs. In 2015, Lin group prepared high quality nitrogen-doped graphene quantum dots using phenylenediamine as precursor [48]. The amino group in phenylenediamine can probably introduce nitrogen into CQDs by reacted with carbonyl group, and induce the formation of nitrogen-doped graphene structure. Herein, a one-step hydrothermal of glucose and phenylenediamine was developedto synthesize N-CQDs without any passivation, and the hydrothermal of glucose and m-phenylenediamine is verified to be a feasible approach to synthesize grapheme structured N-CQDs. The structure, fluorescent properties of so prepared N-CQDs was systematically characterized. The synthesized N-CQDs exhibit low toxicity, good water solubility, considerable quantum yields, and good photostability. Moreover, some applications of the N-CQDs in ion-detection (ferric and chromate ions) and cell-imaging have been committed.

Glucose and m-phenylenediamine were purchased from Aladdin Ltd. (Shanghai, China). The cation solutions of Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+, and Zn2+ in a concentration of 200 mM were formulated from chloride, sulfate and nitrate. All anion solutions (200 mM) of Br−, Cl−, ClO3 −, CNS−, CrO4 2−, F−, HCO3 –, HSO3 −, I−, IO3 −, NO3 –, SO3 2−, and SO4 2− were prepared from sodium or potassium salts. All the chemical reagents were analytical grade and used directly without further purification.

Fourier transform infrared (FTIR) spectroscopy was performed on a Spectrum-400 FT-IR spectrometer with a KBr tableting technique. X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS) was measured on a Kratos Axis Ultra DLD. X-ray diffraction (XRD) patterns were recorded on an XRD-6100 X-ray diffractometer. Raman spectrum was recorded via a Thermo Scientific DXR3xi Raman Imaging Microscopein back scattering geometry at room temperature and 633 nm laser was used as excitation source. High resolution transmission electron microscopy (HRTEM) measurement was carried out on a JEOL JEM-2100F electronic microscope. Ultraviolet–visible (UV–Vis) absorption spectra were obtained on a Cary-100 UV–vis spectrophotometer. The excitation and emission spectra were measured on an F-7000 fluorescent spectrometer with a xenon lamp as excitation resource under ambient conditions. The lifetime of CQDs was recorded on anEdinburghFLS1000spectrometer. Cell-imaging were carried out using an Inverted-BX51 microscope (Olympus) at 355 nm laser.

The N-CQDs were prepared by a facile one-step hydrothermal method. In a typical synthesis, 0.30 g glucose and 0.20 g m-phenylenediamine were dissolved in 20 mL deionized water under ultrasound irradiation. The mixed solution was, then, transferred into a Teflon autoclave. After heating at 190 °C for 12 h, the autoclave was cooled down to room temperature naturally. The obtained solution was centrifuged at 10 000 rpm for 30 min to segregate large particles. The remaining supernatant was further filtered by 0.22 μm hydrophilic filters. The resulting mixture was dried at freezer dryer for 3 days. Thus N-CQDs were prepared.

A relative quantum yield (QY) of N-CQDs was determined with quinine sulfate (dispersed in 0.1 M H2SO4, QY = 54%) as a reference. The following equation was used to calculate the QY [47]:

Among the formula, φ, I, and A are the QY, fluorescence intensity and absorbance of N-CQDs, respectively. φ' , I' , A' are the QY, fluorescence intensity and absorbance of quinine sulfate solution, respectively. n and n , here are both the refraction index of water. Quinine sulfate and N-CQDs solutions were adjusted to a concentration with optical absorbance less than 0.05. The ratio of integrated emission intensity of N-CQDs and that of quinine sulfate solution was used to determine the QY. All data of the solutions were obtained under same specifications.

Followed is the procedure to detect Fe3+ ions using N-CQDs. First, N-CQDs solution in a concentration of 0.002 g·L−1 was prepared by dissolving 0.5 mg N-CQDs in deionized water in a 250 mL volumetric flask. Then 2.0 mL N-CQDs solution so prepared was transferred into a quartz-cuvette and added certain volumes (1–50 μL) of Fe3+ solution. Subsequently, the emission spectra were measured in an excitation silt of 5 nm and emission slit of 10 nm. The detection of CrO4 2− ions are in the same procedure as that of Fe3+ ion. In order to verify the selectivity of N-CQDs to the detection of Fe3+ and CrO4 2− , the emission spectra of some other cations and anions substituting for Fe3+ and CrO4 2−were measured in the same condition as above mentioned.

Cytotoxicity of the N-CQDs was assessed by the standard MTT experiment [49]. First of all, Hela cells (100 μL) were seeded in a 96-well plate with a density of 1 × 105 cells·mL−1. After a 24-hour breeding at 37 °C, the cell medium was discarded, and cells were further treated in Dulbecco’s modified Eagles medium (DMEM) with different concentrations (5–50 μg·mL−1) of N-CQDs in the same volume (200 μL) for extra 24 h. In the final incubation, the cell medium was removed, and 20 μL of MTT (5 mg·mL−1) was added into each well. After another 4 h incubation, the medium was removed, MTT was dissolved in 150 μL DMSO. At the end of incubation, each sample’s optical density (OD) was measured at 490 nm irradiation using an enzyme labeling apparatus (Shanghai, China). The following equation was used to record the cell viability:

The ODTreated and ODControl in the formula is the optical density of the N-CQDs and blank, respectively. Each experiment was performed five times.

The potential of N-CQDs in biolabeling was evaluated by a cellular imaging of Hela cells [50]. In brief, 2.0 mL of Hela cells (4 × 104 cells·mL−1) in DMEM medium were bred in each well and incubated at 37 °C for 24 h in a humid atmosphere with 5% CO2. Then 40 μg·mL−1N-CQDs solution was added to DMEM culture medium. After another incubation of 4 h, DMEM culture medium was wash with buffer solution for three times to remove the free N-CQDs and then fixed using 4% formaldehyde. Subsequently, the fluorescence imaging was recorded by an Inverted-BX51 confocal fluorescence microscope under the excitation of 355 nm laser.
The functional groups of the samples were systematically characterized by FT-IR and XPS.

The FT-IR spectrum of N-CQDs is shown in Fig. 1 with the spectra of glucose and m-phenylenediamine as references. The broad peak around 3400 cm−1 is obviously originated from N-H stretching vibration absorption in amino and OH group [47]. The weak peak at 2260 cm−1 is coincident with the stretching vibration of cyan group [51]. The peak at 1605 cm−1 can be ascribed to the stretching vibration of CN bond or CO, and the peak at 1384 the stretching vibration of C sbnd N bond [52]. The peak at 1137 and those peaks in fingerprint area are all resulted from Ar-H in Benzene. The existence of amino, cyan group, CN and CN bonds indicates that nitrogen has been chemically doped into CQDs.

To further elaborate the doping of nitrogen and the chemical composition of CQDs, XPS of CQDs was performed and the results are presented in Fig. 2 . A survey scan was first conducted from 0 to 800 eV (Fig. 2a). The primary peaks at 285, 400 and 532 eV are attributed to the 1s peak values of carbon, nitrogen, and oxygen, respectively [47]; and the atomic concentrations were 75.69, 10.51 and 13.79%, respectively. The atomic concentration of nitrogen in CQDs is greatly lower than in m-phenylenediamine, this implies that m-phenylenediamine has reacted with glucose and nitrogen has been chemically doped into CQDs. Deconvolution of the nitrogen 1speak (Fig. 2b) exhibits three peaks at 399.3, 399.6 and 400.8 eV, which are the characteristic peaks of pyridine N, amino N and N-H, respectively [53,54]. The spectrum of carbon 1s peak can be dissolved into five peaks at 283.69, 284.20, 284.68, 285.65 and 285.77 eV, which are the characteristic peaks of CN, CC/CH, CH, CN and CO/CO, respectively [24,55]. Oxygen 1s peak gives three peaks at 532.18, 533.09 and 533.84 eV, which are originated from COH, OCO* and CO, respectively [54,55]. The results of XPS are in accordance with those of FT-IR, this further demonstrates that the CQDs have been chemically doped with nitrogen.
The structure and crystal size of N-CQDs are characterized by Raman, XRD and TEM.

A Raman spectrum (Fig. 3 a) was first recorded to confirm the crystal structure and size of the N-CQDs. The spectrum exhibits the characteristic Raman band sat 1357 and 1565 cm−1, conventionally labeled as D band and G band, respectively [56].D band concerns the crystalline size and defects, while G band is related to the graphite phase of the sample. The high intensity of G band over D band and the absence of 2D band clearly demonstrate that the CQD well crystallized in graphene structure without stacking. The ratio, ID/IG, is in proportion to 1/La (ID represents the intensity of D band, IG the intensity of G band and La the crystalline lateral size), the crystalline lateral size calculated based on this relation is ca. 8 nm [56].

The XRD pattern of N-CQDs (Fig. 3b) shows a broad diffraction peak with a maximum at 23°. This is coincident with the diffraction of grapheme. The size of N-CQDs calculated from Debye-Scherrer formula is ca. 8 nm, agree well with the size calculated from Raman spectrum [57].

As shown in Fig. 3c, the morphology of the N-CQDs was revealed in TEM picture. The HRTEM photograph of N-CQDs was taken and the photograph is shown in Fig. 3d. The distinct lattice fringes in the photograph reveal that the N-CQDs are well crystallized. The fringe spacing (inset in Fig. 3d) is about 0.212 nm, coincident with the (100) interplanar spacing of graphite [49,53]. A statistical result of the grain size of N-CQD based on the HRTEM photograph indicates the grain size is in the scope of 6–15 nm, and the average size is also ca. 8 nm.

The results of Raman, XRD, and TEM verify each other. This indicates nitrogen has been chemically doped into CQDs, and nitrogen-doping has little influence on the structure of graphite phase, and the N-CQDs are a collection of graphene in a size of ca.8 nm.
The fluorescence of N-CQDs is basically luminescence of semiconductor. In order to figure out the band structure of N-CQDs, the UV–Vis absorption spectrum of N-CQDs was first measured.

The absorption spectrum and Tacu plot base on the absorption spectrum are presented in Fig. 4 . The absorption spectrum of N-CQDs (Fig. 4a) exhibits four main absorption peaks, a typical π-π* transition of CC at 220 and 270 nm [58], a distinct n-π* absorption of CO of carbonyl and CN at 290 nm [59–61], and a band gap transition absorption peak at 370 nm [60]. The band-gap of N-CQDs can be evaluated from Tacu plot (Fig. 4b). Fig. 4b shows a good linear fit when r (r is the power exponent of αhν) equals to 2, this demonstrates the N-CQDs are direct bandgap semiconductor, and possess a band gap of about 3.43 eV [60].

The excitation, emission spectra of N-CQDs were measured and the results are shown in Fig. 5 a. The excitation spectrum, monitored by 460 nm emission, consists of a broad band with a maximum at 320 nm, which is resulted from the band gap transition. The emission spectrum, excited by 320 nm, exhibits blue emission at 460 nm, and the full width of half maximum of it is ca. 70 nm. N-CQDs always exhibit excitation dependent emission, so the emission spectra of N-CQDs, excited by light from 290 to 440 nm, are recorded and the results are shown in Fig. 5b. The emission intensity and wavelength are both dependent on the excitation wavelength. The emission intensity increases gradually and reaches its maximum at an excitation wavelength of 320 nm, and then decreases continuously with the increase of excitation wavelength. Similar to previous works, the emission of the N-CQDs exhibits an obvious excitation dependent performance, namely, the emission wavelength shifts to longer wavelength with the increase of excitation wavelength. The maximum emission wavelength as a function of excitation wavelength is shown in Fig. 5c. As excitation wavelength increases, the red-shift of emission wavelength increases in an S-shape curve. This excitation dependent property is due to the multiple surface emissive trap statuses of N-CQDs [62,63]. Using quinine sulfate solution as reference, the QY of N-CQDs is determined to be 17.5%.

Besides, the emission of N-CQDs owns relatively stable emission intensity under different conditions. After an hour irradiation, the PL intensity exhibits nearly no decrease at all (Fig. 6 a). The increase of temperature only slightly decreases the PL intensity of N-CQDs (Fig. 6b). The PL intensity keeps nearly the same at a broad range of ionic strength from 0 to 0.5 mM NaCl. The emission stability of the N-CQDs provides a reliable platform for ion-detection.

The PL intensity of N-CQDs is extremely sensitive to certain minute perturbation, therefore, they are excellent candidates for the detection of certain perturbation. The fluorescent spectra of the N-CQDs under a slight disturbance of cations were monitored and the results are shown in Fig. 7 a. The PL intensity of N-CQDs solutions (2 μg·mL−1), added with different cations (Ag+, Ba2+, Ca2+, Cd2+, Co2+, Cu2+, Fe2+, Fe3+, K+, Mg2+, Ni2+, Pb2+ and Zn2+) in concentration of 5 mM, clearly demonstrates that the PL of N-CQDs solution was 98% quenched by Fe3+, therefore, the N-CQDs are a kind of superior fluorescent probe for Fe3+ fluorescent detection. The PL intensity vs. Fe3+ concentration (Fig. 7b and c) presents that the PL intensity monotonously decreases with the increase of Fe3+ concentration and can be well fitted into a linear equation, F = -1221.8 × [Fe3+] + 1644.7 (F represents PL intensity), with an R2 (R2 represents correlation coefficient) of 0.99 in the scope of 0.6–1.0 mM.

A possible mechanism of fluorescent quenching of N-CQDs by Fe3+ was investigated according to Stern-Volmer equation [64]:
1 +
K
SV
×
Q
=
F 0
/ F
where K SV is the quenching constant, [Q] is the concentrations of quenching agent, F and F0 are the fluorescent intensities of N-CQDs with and without quenching agent, respectively. As demonstrated in Fig. 7d, F0/F vs. the concentration of Fe3+ can be linearly fitted with a correlationcoefficient of 1.2 in the scope of 0–1 mM. A dynamic quenching mechanism always possesses a K SV in the scope of 102-103, while the KSV of quenching agent of Fe3+is only 1.2, far more less than that of dynamic quenching, so the quenching mechanism is obviously a static quenching mechanism [64,65].

The PL intensity of N-CQDs solutions (2 μg·mL−1), added different anions (Br−, Cl−, ClO3 −, CNS−, CrO4 2−, F−, HCO3 –, HSO3 −, I−, IO3 −, NO3 –, SO3 2−, and SO4 2−) in concentration of 5 mM is shown in Fig. 8 a, which reveals that the N-CQDs are also an excellent PL quencher of CrO4 2− (98% quenching). The PL intensity decrease with the increase of CrO4 2− concentration (Fig. 8b, 8c). A linear fitting of PL intensity and concentration of CrO4 2− gives an equation: F = −1335.1 × [CrO4 2−] + 2895.9, with an R2 of 0.99 in a scope of 0.5–1.0 mM. The correlationcoefficient of a linear fitting of F0/F vs. the concentration of CrO4 2− is 4.2, far more less than 102-103, so the quenching mechanism of CrO4 2− is also a static quenching model. Moreover, the limit of detection (LOD) of Fe3+ and CrO4 2− was calculated by the following equation: LOD = 3σ/K [66] and the LODs of Fe3+ and CrO4 2− are 9.11 and 8.34 μM, respectively. As a reference, Table 1 and 2
list different response systems for detecting Fe3+ and CrO4 2−.

The absorption spectra of all analytes were measured to figure out the ions detection selectivity of N-CQDs. As shown in Fig. 9 , Fe3+ and CrO4 2− show strong absorbance at 320 nm, while other ions have very low absorbance. The absorption peak of Fe3+ (and CrO4 2−) and that of N-CQDs overlap each other (Fig. 10 ). Therefore, Fe3+ (or CrO4 2−) will significantly quench the emission of N-CQDs. This indicates the quenching of N-CQDs by CrO4 2− or Fe3+ is due to an inner filter effect. In order to further confirm the ways of the quenching mechanism, PL decay curves of N-CQDs solution and those of N-CQDs solutions added with Fe3+ and CrO4 2− are presented in Fig. 11 . All the decay curves can be well fitted into a single exponent function regardless of the addition of Fe3+ or CrO4 2−, and the lifetimes are all nearly 7 ns, which means that there is no energy transfer from N-CQDs to Fe3+ or CrO4 2−. These results further verify the quenching mechanism is based on an inner filter effect.

Owing to their excellent light stability, biocompatibility, CQDs have long been candidates in cell-imaging [19,73,81–86]. HeLa cells here were used to evaluate the vitro cell toxicity by the standard MTT approach [50]. The cell viability of HeLa cells treated with N-CQDs from 5 to 50 μg·mL−1 was compared with that of the cells cultivated in glucose solution (Fig. 12 a). The results say that the N-CQDs possess low cytotoxicity under lower concentration. The surviving HeLa cells, which was bred under a definite concentration of N-CQDs (40 μg·mL−1), were imaged by a confocal microscope under 355 nm laser excitation and bright field lighting. The confocal picture exhibits blue-black emission in Hela cell (Fig. 12b). It is obvious that the emission is primarily situated in the cytoplasmic region, which means the N-CQDs can enter cell through the cell membrane.

N-CQDs were successfully synthesized by a one-step hydrothermal of glucose and m-phenylenediamine. The N-CQDs consist of well crystalline graphene in a grain size of ca. 8 nm. The N-CQDs emit strong blue emission under the irradiation of ultraviolet, and the emission red shift with the increase of excitation wavelength. Fe3+ and CrO4 2− have been efficient quenchers of the N–CQD emission, so N-CQDs are good candidates for fluorescent probes of Fe3+ and CrO4 2− ions. The quenching mechanism of CrO4 2− and Fe3+to N-CQDs is determined to be a static quenching by internal filtration approach. Cytotoxicity and cell-imaging experiments reveal that the N-CQDs have good biocompatibility, are potential candidates of cell-labeling reagent.

Tong-Yang Shen: Conceptualization, Methodology, Formal analysis. Pei-Yun Jia: Data curation, Visualization, Investigation, Writing - original draft. Da-Shu Chen: Writing - review & editing. Li-Na Wang: Resources.
